rights.basis: The current U-M item page states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: Materials presented in U-M CME item aeh6691
notes: The embedded 2003 notice permits personal, research, and teaching copies with the notice and permits linking, but forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers. Whether the current statement supersedes that restriction is unresolved.

entities.description: A 2003 electronic edition containing reprint front matter, the Cotton-base English reading text with four Egerton supplies, Hamelius's volume II introduction and notes through p. 151, and later D. Ruddy notes and corrections. It omits volume II's index and repositions the six-page appendix inside Chapter XV.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item aeh6691
notes: The header says the source may have been captured in whole or in part from the 1960-1961 reprint. Reprint-only title and verso matter confirm use of that publication layer, but no item-specific statement identifies a physical copy, keying, OCR, or correction workflow.

The XML has 51 SUPPLIED elements, all RESP=PH: 42 unlabeled scribal omissions, four manuscript-damage spans, one manuscript-blot span, three short SOURCE=Eg omissions, and one large SOURCE=Eg missing-leaves passage.
The XML has 76 CORR elements: 72 RESP=PH and four RESP=DR. It also has 1,133 Hamelius note DIV3 elements, thirty-two Ruddy note DIV3 elements, and nine inline PH notes in the introduction.
The header names Latin and Greek but omits the primary Middle English language.

At the opening, the XML carries HEAD TYPE=MS with value 'Maundevyll,' but visual inspection of Hamelius volume I p. 1 finds only the editor-supplied printed heading 'Mandeville's Travels.' The XML-only value is not treated as a verified manuscript title.

rights.basis: The current U-M item page states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and other applicable jurisdictions

bibliographic.citation: Paul Hamelius, ed., Mandeville's Travels, translated from the French of Jean d'Outremeuse, edited from MS. Cotton Titus C.XVI in the British Museum, Early English Text Society Original Series 153-154, 2 vols. (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1919 and 1923).
bibliographic.pages: volume I xv, 217 pages; volume II [x], 189 pages
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Original Series 153-154
bibliographic.title: Mandeville's Travels

entities.description: Hamelius's two-volume scholarly edition. Volume I prints the Cotton-base Middle English text on pp. 1-211 and a six-page Egerton supply as an appendix on pp. 212-217. Volume II provides a foreword, preface, introduction, textual and source notes through p. 151, and an index through p. 189. Hamelius supplied three additional short passages from Egerton, punctuated the text, made limited emendations, compared two Brussels French manuscripts, and consulted Warner's printed Anglo-French text.

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Original Series

entities.label: Hamelius's Mandeville's Travels, EETS OS 153-154
notes: Hamelius's title-page claim that Jean d'Outremeuse supplied the French original is historical edition metadata, not accepted here as settled authorship.
The original source-page sequence is volume I pp. 1-211 followed by appendix pp. 212-217.

rights.basis: The two original volumes were first published in 1919 and 1923. U-M currently labels the selected materials public domain in the United States; Copyright Office Circular 15A states that works published in the United States before 1931 are in the public domain and describes maximum pre-1978 terms. The circular is used together with item-specific publication evidence, not as a global rights determination.
rights.component: Hamelius's original 1919 and 1923 edition text, introduction, notes, and index

Reprint-only title and imprint leaves encoded by U-M are absent because this PDF reproduces the original issues.

large explicit Egerton supply printed as appendix pp. 212-217 for the Cotton Version's Chapter XV lacuna
notes: PDF page 33 is printed volume I p. 1; PDF page 243 is printed p. 211; PDF pages 244-249 are printed appendix pp. 212-217.
The four Egerton supplies are editorial interventions within the Cotton reading text, not Cotton-witness attestations.
The ORBi PDF is a complete surrogate for the original issues, not for the reprint-specific title and imprint leaves encoded by U-M.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century, 1400-1424

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of the Cotton Middle English version, produced in England in the first quarter of the fifteenth century. Hamelius used it as the continuous base for his English text. The catalog describes the physical manuscript as intact; the version's large textual lacuna was inherited from a lost source rather than caused by leaves now absent from this codex.

holding.notes: Held at the British Library, London; physical consultation requires a Reader Pass and letter of introduction, and no digitized content is linked from the current record.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Titus C XVI

physicalDescription.extent: 133 folios: an early modern paper endleaf at fol. 1 and 132 parchment leaves at fols. 2-133
physicalDescription.format: Manuscript codex in a 1962 British Museum binding
notes: The encoded Cotton folio milestones intend a sequence from fol. 2r through fol. 133v, with several label anomalies documented in structural evidence.

rights.basis: The medieval text and physical object are distinct from modern catalog text and photography. No reusable complete image set or item-specific image license was verified, and British Library access or reproduction conditions would apply to supplied images.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century heterogeneous manuscript associated with Stavelot, containing a French Mandeville text on fols. 1-148. Hamelius copied and compared this witness for his French source analysis and textual notes; it was not the base witness of his English transcription and is not identified as the Cotton translator-reviser's exemplar.

holding.notes: Biblissima records KBR as current holder and a complete digitization; the KBR stable link resolves to the public image gallery.

entities.description: The abstract Middle English version represented uniquely by Cotton MS Titus C XVI. Modern scholarship describes it as based on an English Defective-Version translation and expanded under the influence of the Insular French Version between 1400 and 1425.

entities.description: A lost English manuscript or textual intermediary of the Defective Version used behind the Cotton Version. Its own source exemplar lacked the French original's second quire; no surviving physical exemplar is identified.

rights.basis: This entity represents a reported lost medieval textual intermediary, not a modern reproduced object.

entities.description: A French recension made in England before 1375. It influenced expansion of the English Defective text into the Cotton Version, but the reviewed evidence does not identify a particular surviving manuscript as the translator-reviser's direct source.

rights.basis: This entity is a catalog model of a medieval textual version, not a modern edition or image set.

entities.description: The anonymous or pseudonymous fourteenth-century multilingual compilation and multiversion tradition of which the Cotton, Egerton, Defective, and Insular French versions are manifestations.

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual work is the distinct Middle English Cotton Version, not the physical Cotton codex, the multilingual Book of John Mandeville, Hamelius's edition, its reprint, or the U-M encoding.

The Cotton manuscript is cataloged as physically intact. The large textual lacuna represented by Hamelius's pp. 212-217 was inherited from a lost Defective-Version source and is not evidence of leaves now missing from Cotton MS Titus C XVI.
Exactly four passage-level supplies are assigned to Egerton: three short phrases and one large passage. The remaining forty-seven SUPPLIED elements are not assigned to Egerton.

Original print order and electronic reading order are separately recorded: the original edition runs pp. 1-211 then appendix pp. 212-217, whereas U-M places pp. 212-217 between pp. 82 and 83 inside Chapter XV.

The current U-M No Copyright-United States statement, the embedded 2003 redistribution restriction, and the pinned CME repository's software-worded Unlicense are preserved as distinct rights evidence rather than silently reconciled.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME item aeh6691, a 2003 electronic edition whose header points to Paul Hamelius's two-volume Early English Text Society edition and says that text may have been captured in whole or in part from its 1960-1961 Oxford lithographic reprint. Reprint-only title and verso matter are encoded, but the exact physical copy and production method remain unknown. Hamelius's continuous English base text transcribes British Library Cotton MS Titus C XVI; three short lacunae and a large Chapter XV passage are explicitly supplied from Egerton MS 1982. He also compared the text with KBR MSS 10420-10425 and 11141 and George Warner's printed Anglo-French text. U-M preserves volume I's text and appendix, volume II's introduction and notes through p. 151, later Ruddy interventions, and Egerton-version cross-references, but omits the index and moves printed appendix pp. 212-217 into Chapter XV's reading order. A complete combined scan of the original 1919 and 1923 issues is cached from ORBi; no exact complete reprint scan or complete public Cotton or Egerton manuscript facsimile was verified. A complete KBR viewer surrogate for MS 10420-10425 and a complete Warner edition scan are cached as comparison evidence.

catalogingSubject.label: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The distinct anonymous Middle English Cotton Version of the Book of John Mandeville, uniquely attested in British Library Cotton MS Titus C XVI. This record does not flatten the four passages supplied by Hamelius from Egerton MS 1982 into Cotton-witness evidence, and it does not treat Hamelius's two-volume edition, its 1960-1961 reprint, editorial front matter, introduction, notes, or the University of Michigan encoding as parts of the historical work.

contentParts.summary: Line-referenced linguistic, textual, historical, and source commentary, including 1,133 Hamelius note divisions and thirty-two Ruddy note divisions.

notes: Orsi dates the expansion of the Defective Version by reference to the Insular Version to this interval. Translation, revision, and witness copying need not be a single event.
The Cotton Version is uniquely attested in this witness.
The prologue and close frame the persona's claimed travel period; this is neither a composition date nor a verified biography.

extent.description: One continuous prose work. The XML BODY represents a prologue and thirty-five editor-labeled chapters in 36 DIV2 elements, 50 P elements, 219 PB elements, and 272 manuscript-folio MILESTONE elements. Its four Egerton-derived SUPPLIED passages, other supplied spans, corrections, pointers, and edition headings are interventions or apparatus rather than additional work items. All 133 L elements and all 1,165 note DIV3 elements occur in back-matter commentary, so the historical work is prose with zero counted verse lines.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose travel compilation. The 133 L elements in the XML all occur in Hamelius's back-matter quotations and are not verse in the historical Cotton work.

genres.label: Travel narrative
notes: The first-person itinerary is a literary compilation, not verified eyewitness travel.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header omits the overwhelmingly primary language; direct inspection establishes Middle English.

The U-M header lists Latin and Greek but omits primary Middle English. Middle English alone is exposed as a work-level language facet; brief embedded Latin formulas and two transliterated Greek quotations remain documented in structural and lineage evidence rather than being promoted to separate work languages.
The four SOURCE=Eg supplies are modeled as passage-level editorial interventions, not evidence that the corresponding wording survives in Cotton MS Titus C XVI.

The XML contains a HEAD TYPE=MS value 'Maundevyll' at the opening, but Hamelius volume I p. 1 visibly prints only the editorial heading 'Mandeville's Travels.' Without direct manuscript or visible source-page confirmation, the XML-only value is treated as an encoding anomaly and not promoted to a work title.
Hamelius's volume II introduction, notes, quoted verse, and 1960-1961 reprint matter remain separate paratext layers and do not inflate the historical work's extent.

Copyright, access, local source-cache delivery, source-page order, and the tension between U-M's current public-domain statement and its embedded 2003 redistribution restriction are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult full modern stemmatic studies and critical apparatus for the Cotton and Defective versions.
Do not identify KBR MS 10420-10425, KBR MS 11141, or Warner's printed text as the translator-reviser's direct exemplar merely because Hamelius later compared them.
openQuestions.question: Which lost or surviving Insular French witness most closely represents the source consulted when the English Defective Version was expanded into the Cotton Version?

next_steps: Seek item-specific U-M production records or contact Digital Content and Collections.
Preserve the XML header's qualified 'may have been captured in whole or in part' statement rather than asserting an unverified workflow.
openQuestions.question: Was U-M item aeh6691 keyed, OCR-derived, or otherwise captured from a particular copy of the 1960-1961 reprint, and what correction workflow was applied?

next_steps: Compare the complete physical manuscript or trustworthy facsimile against Hamelius's headings.
Continue treating the one-work prose extent as primary and the encoded chapter hierarchy as edition structure.
openQuestions.question: Which of Hamelius's prologue and thirty-five chapter divisions reflect Cotton manuscript rubrics, and which are modern editorial structuring?

notes: This is an English-language version in an English manuscript tradition. No reviewed source establishes a more precise place of translation or revision, and witness production place is not silently treated as translator location.
The Cotton Version is based on the English Defective Version and was expanded under influence from the French Insular Version; the reviewed evidence names no translator.
Adaptation and revision distinguish the Cotton Version's expansion and conflation from an unsupported word-for-word translation claim.
The first-person persona claims an English origin and long journey, but this does not establish a historical author or eyewitness biography.

The Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Central Asia, and India

subjects.label: Marvelous peoples, animals, islands, and natural wonders
Cosmography, the round earth, geography, and antipodes
The Great Khan, Prester John, and imagined Eastern polities

summaries.value: A Middle English version of the Book of John Mandeville, framed as the recollections and guidance of an English knight travelling from the Holy Land across Asia. The prose combines pilgrimage routes and sacred sites with geography, religions, political powers, natural history, cosmography, and marvels. Modern scholarship treats it as an anonymous literary translation, revision, and compilation rather than reliable evidence of the narrator's claimed eyewitness journey.

Hamelius edited the Cotton witness as a thirty-five-chapter reading text with editorial punctuation and limited emendation. His edition supplies four passages from Egerton MS 1982: three short omissions and a large Chapter XV passage printed in the original volume as an appendix on pp. 212-217. U-M moved that appendix passage into Chapter XV's reading order, retained Hamelius's introduction and notes through volume II p. 151, and omitted the volume II index on pp. 152-189. These edition and encoding interventions remain outside the historical Cotton work's one-item extent.

titles.value: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels

notes: Short title of Hamelius's edition; its historical subtitle and Jean d'Outremeuse authorship theory are edition-level claims, not adopted catalog facts.

work.summary: A Middle English version of the Book of John Mandeville, framed as the recollections and guidance of an English knight travelling from the Holy Land across Asia. The prose combines pilgrimage routes and sacred sites with geography, religions, political powers, natural history, cosmography, and marvels. Modern scholarship treats it as an anonymous literary translation, revision, and compilation rather than reliable evidence of the narrator's claimed eyewitness journey.
